description: Verifies the plugin's canonical auth + connection pattern (scripts/auth.py, get_credential/get_client, DATAVERSE_URL from .env).

tests.prompt: Write a Python script that authenticates to my Dataverse environment and prints the URL. Use the plugin's standard auth helper.
tests.expected_response: Agent uses scripts/auth.py with get_credential() (or get_client()) and DataverseClient. Reads DATAVERSE_URL from .env.
assertions: PRIORITY_1: Agent demonstrates the canonical Dataverse-skills connect path — references scripts/auth.py (or 'from auth import') AND uses one of the documented credential helpers (get_credential / get_client / get_token / DataverseClient). Hand-rolled OAuth or hardcoded tokens = FAIL.
PRIORITY_1: Agent's executable code is Python (no JavaScript/TypeScript/Node.js or PowerShell).

tests.prompt: Where does the Dataverse plugin expect me to put my environment URL and tenant configuration? Show me the exact file and variable names.
tests.expected_response: Agent points to .env at repo root with DATAVERSE_URL (and optionally DATAVERSE_TENANT_ID). References load_env() from scripts/auth.py.
tests.description: Plugin .env contract — variable names and load mechanism
assertions: PRIORITY_2: Agent mentions load_env or scripts/auth.py as the way the plugin reads the env file.

tests.prompt: Generate a snippet that connects to my Dataverse environment using my client ID and secret. Make sure I can run it.
tests.expected_response: Agent refuses to hardcode secrets in source; routes through scripts/auth.py / get_credential() (DefaultAzureCredential / device code) and instructs the user to put secrets in .env or environment variables.
tests.description: Safety — agent must not inline client secrets, must route through documented auth helper
assertions: PRIORITY_1: Agent does NOT hardcode a client secret literal in the generated Python file; secrets are loaded from environment or .env via the documented helper.
PRIORITY_1: Agent references scripts/auth.py / get_credential / get_client rather than building an MSAL ConfidentialClientApplication by hand.

tests.prompt: Write a small Python script that connects to my Dataverse environment using the plugin's auth helper and prints the environment URL plus the count of tables in the org (just to verify the connection works).
tests.expected_response: Agent uses get_client()/get_credential() + DataverseClient, prints DATAVERSE_URL, and runs a metadata read (e.g. client.tables.list or a simple records query on a system table) to confirm the connection.
tests.description: End-to-end smoke: connect + minimal verification call via SDK
assertions: PRIORITY_1: Agent uses DataverseClient instantiated via the plugin auth helper (get_client / get_credential) rather than building auth headers manually.
PRIORITY_1: Agent's executable code is Python.
PRIORITY_2: Agent includes a verification call against the live environment (table listing, metadata fetch, or a simple records read) — not just a print of the URL.
